title: Taxol Extract from Lauth Sala (West Himalayan Yew) for Cancer Therapy
author: Subedi Prakash
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Thirty-one years ago, promising new chemotherapy medications were introduced. Taxol or paclitaxel was extremely potent. Paclitaxel disrupts microtubule dynamics, causing tubulin polymerization and cell death. Membrane phosphor-glycoprotein drug-efflux pumps and tubulin structural alterations generate paclitaxel resistance. Taxaceae contains Taxus contorta, the West Himalayan yew. A medicinal tree from the Himalayas. This evergreen tree grows from 1700–2600 MSL in Nepal. Native civilizations use it to treat fever, coughing, colds, and discomfort. Cancer medicine paclitaxel derives from West Himalayan yew tree bark or leaves. Taxol's increased demand threatens yew trees. Researchers are employing Lauth Sala (West Himalayan Yew) as a Taxol substitute to remedy this. In this study, Lauth Sala bark or leaves were ground, soaked in ethanol, then separated and purified using column chromatography to produce Taxol. TLC determined taxol purity. Bark provided 0.11% pure dry Taxol, whereas leaves yielded 0.39%, according to the study. Bark provided less pure dry Taxol than leaves from the West Himalayan yew. According to this study, Lauth Sala may replace Taxol. Paclitaxel produces neurotoxicity, hypersensitivity, and haematological damage. Schedule and dose determine toxicity. Paclitaxel cures malignancies of the breast, ovary, lung, head, and neck.

keywords: Taxol extraction; Lauth Sala (Taxus contorta); Taxus species; Anticancer compounds; Natural product isolation; Chemotherapy alternatives; Sustainable drug sources
